Capt. Joseph St. John of Norwalk

enslaved_persons.name: Peter
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved man. Listed in 1786 estate inventory.

enslaved_persons.name: Phyllis
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved woman. Listed in 1786 estate inventory.

military_service.notes: Capt. 1st Co. Norwalk May 1761; Major 9th Regt. May 1775; Major 1st Batt. Col. Whiting Nov 1776; Lt. Col. 9th Regt. May 1777. On committee of Articles of Confederation 1777.

bio.title: Lieutenant Colonel
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Lt. Col. Stephen Saint John Esq.

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave #44152495. Stone: 'Lt Col command of the 9th Reg of Foot Guard of the State of Connecticut who departed this life May the 9th 1785, in the 50th year of his age.'

narrative_blocks.header: Led Connecticut's 9th Regiment of Foot as Lt.
narrative_blocks.body: Stephen Saint John rose to Lieutenant Colonel in command of the 9th Regiment of Foot, Guard of the State of Connecticut — a rank inscribed on his tombstone in East Norwalk Historical Cemetery. His family's marriage alliances connected two of Norwalk's most prominent dynasties: the Saint Johns married into the Fitches across two consecutive generations.

narrative_blocks.header: He sent no substitute; his brother did
narrative_blocks.body: A Connecticut selectman and Revolutionary officer, he led the colony’s 9th Regiment of Foot. When the war came, an enslaved man named Dover was sent to fight in the Continental Army in place of his brother — while his own household still held two people, Peter and Phyllis, at his death.

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave #74217046 area. East Norwalk Historical Cemetery.

cross_connections.display_label: Married first cousin Nancy Susan Marvin — both Hooker descendants
Thaddeus Betts served alongside him in CT House, 1815

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave. Saint Pauls Cemetery Norwalk CT. Died Jul 23, 1846 (newspaper gives 'Saturday evening' = Jul 25 — minor discrepancy). 'Benj Isaacs Esq, so well-known as a leading citizen.'

narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin Isaacs died suddenly of cholera morbus on July 23, 1846, prompting the Bridgeport Republican Farmer to eulogize him as a 'leading citizen' who had served as representative in both branches of the Connecticut Legislature and Judge of Probate and County Court for many years. His wife Frances died 'very suddenly' eight weeks later on September 13, 1846 — the obituary noting simply that she was the 'widow of Judge Isaacs.'

narrative_blocks.header: Had a daughter born in Mexican California, 1828
narrative_blocks.body: John Isaacs was born in Norwalk, Connecticut, son of Isaac Scudder Isaacs and Susannah Saint John Isaacs (H01239, a Hooker descendant), and married Elizabeth Ann Saint John on October 7-8, 1821. His daughter Mary Etta was born in San Francisco on March 20, 1828 — when it was still called Yerba Buena under Mexican rule — placing his family among the very earliest documented New England visitors to Alta California, more than twenty years before the 1849 Gold Rush.

notable.notable_blurb: Bridgeport founder, physician, turnpike company founder

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founder of the Borough of Bridgeport
narrative_blocks.detail: His estate was distributed in 1805 to his widow Sarah and their children. A note in the research suggests a John Cannon (possibly his son) founded the Bridgeport Bank in 1806 and served as its president from 1823 until 1827 — the probate timeline makes it likely this was the son John Jr. (HD2793) rather than the physician himself. In the 1790 US Census, one enslaved person was enumerated in his household. His daughter Antoinette married U.S. Senator Thaddeus Betts; his daughter Sarah married back into the St. John family. (St. John Genealogy; Bridgeport historical records)
narrative_blocks.body: John Cannon Jr. moved from Norwalk to Bridgeport (then called Newfield) around 1790. He was involved in founding the Newfield Fire Company (1796), helped incorporate the Borough of Bridgeport (1800), and founded the Bridgeport & Newtown Turnpike Company (1801). With William Peet he ran a saddlery business as Cannon & Peet.

With the tanner William Peet he ran the saddlery firm Cannon & Peet on Broad Street, then helped raise the subscription that bought Newfield its first fire engine in 1796 — fifteen dollars of his own among the names. He died in office as president of the Bridgeport Bank.

narrative_blocks.header: One enslaved person in his household
narrative_blocks.body: The 1790 census enumerated one enslaved person in his Norwalk household; later Bridgeport records name a man, Cato Cannon, among those he held. The turnpike-builder and bank president was, like many Connecticut founders of his day, a slaveholder.

cross_connections.display_label: shares a Peet family tie worth tracing

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-nine in Wilton; three children left
narrative_blocks.body: Nancy Saint John married Matthew Marvin at twenty-three and moved from Norwalk to Wilton's Pimpawaug valley, where his family had been anchored since his great-grandfather settled there around 1730. She died in September 1808 at thirty-nine, leaving three children between the ages of five and fifteen.

narrative_blocks.header: Revolutionary soldier; Marvin family held Wilton homesite four
narrative_blocks.body: Matthew Marvin, a Revolutionary War soldier, lived on the same ancestral homesite in Wilton's Pimpawaug valley that his great-grandfather had selected as early as 1730, following an Indian trail from Norwalk. Four generations of Marvins served the Wilton Congregational Church on that same land — including his grandson Matthew Marvin, Esquire and great-grandson Honorable Charles Marvin, both deacons.

narrative_blocks.header: Fourth-generation Wilton deacon; last Marvin on homesite
narrative_blocks.body: Charles M. Marvin was the fourth and final generation to anchor the Marvin homesite in Wilton's Pimpawaug valley — from the family's arrival around 1730 through his own death in 1883. The Wilton church records note both he and his uncle Matthew Marvin, Esquire as deacons, crediting the family with 'a powerful influence in promoting the prosperity of Church, Parish and Town.'

cross_connections.display_label: Married first cousin Charles Saint John — both Hooker descendants

narrative_blocks.body: Stephen Saint John married Sarah Fitch, daughter of Colonel Thomas 'Yankee Doodle' Fitch V — a generation after his father H00479 had married Ann Fitch, Sarah's aunt. Two consecutive generations of Saint Johns had wed into the Fitch family, completing a dynasty of Norwalk intermarriage spanning governor and colonel alike.

burialCemetery.name: East Norwalk Historical Cemetery
burialCemetery.access_notes: Sometimes locked; managed by local taxing district
features: First Settlers of Norwalk Memorial (lists 31 individuals from 1649)
Grave of Thomas 'Yankee Doodle' Fitch IV (1724-1795)
burialCemetery.notes: Oldest cemetery in Norwalk; triangle-shaped; near East Norwalk railway station. Mary Willet Hooker Buckingham buried here, near center of burying ground near East Norwalk railway station.
